metadata.summary: Production multi-agent system with a central async message bus supporting direct, broadcast, and request communication; switchable execution strategies (hierarchical, parallel, sequential) at runtime.

content.title: Production Multi-Agent System with Message Bus
content.summary: A central MessageBus handles async agent-to-agent communication via three modes: direct (point-to-point), broadcast (fan-out to all except sender), and request. ProductionMultiAgentSystem wraps the bus with three switchable execution strategies — hierarchical (orchestrator plan + worker dispatch + synthesis), parallel (asyncio.gather fan-out), and sequential (pipeline chain) — selected at run_task() call time.

why.p: Simple synchronous multi-agent patterns break in production: one slow worker blocks all downstream agents; there is no audit trail of inter-agent messages; cost per run is invisible until the bill arrives; and the coordination logic is tightly coupled to the strategy (hierarchical vs parallel vs sequential). A production system must decouple communication (the bus) from coordination strategy (the run_task() method), maintain a message history for debugging, and support async execution to prevent worker blocking. The three strategies cover the most common production shapes without requiring a rewrite for each use case.

list.item: Production systems that need to switch coordination strategy at runtime without rewriting agent code — the strategy parameter selects hierarchical, parallel, or sequential.
Systems requiring an audit trail of every inter-agent message (sender, receiver, content, type) for compliance or debugging.
Async environments where worker blocking is unacceptable — the bus decouples send from receive, preventing circular waits.
Cost-sensitive deployments where per-agent token usage must be attributed and budget-gated.
Simple two-agent pipelines — the bus adds subscription setup overhead with no value for point-to-point synchronous chains.
Prototypes where iteration speed matters more than auditability — set up a plain ManagerAgent or CollaborativeGroup first.
Synchronous-only environments where asyncio is unavailable or forbidden — the bus is async-native.

section.name: message-bus-implementation
section.p: MessageBus holds a subscribers dict (agent_name → list of async handlers) and a message_history list. subscribe() registers a handler for an agent. send() dispatches by CommunicationType: BROADCAST fans out to all except sender; DIRECT sends to the specific receiver only. All messages are appended to message_history before dispatch.

rule.statement: All inter-agent messages MUST use structured content (JSON-serializable dict or validated schema). Free-form string messages MUST NOT be sent via the bus in production.
rule.rationale: Free-form text increases parsing failures at the receiving agent. Structured messages allow schema validation before dispatch and make the message_history machine-readable for cost attribution and replay debugging.

rule.statement: Every agent handler MUST be wrapped with asyncio.wait_for(handler(message), timeout=X). Never await a handler without a timeout in a production bus.
rule.rationale: A single slow or hung agent handler blocks the entire send() coroutine. Without a timeout, one agent can deadlock the bus. Set timeouts at the bus level so individual agent failures degrade gracefully.

example.title: MessageBus with broadcast and direct dispatch

section.name: execution-strategies
section.p: ProductionMultiAgentSystem wraps the bus with three switchable execution strategies. All three share the same _create_plan() and _synthesize_results() helpers — only the dispatch logic differs.

example.title: ProductionMultiAgentSystem with three strategies

antipattern.description: Context leakage: agents share no memory by default, but accidentally shared global state (e.g. a Python dict) can cause race conditions in async parallel execution.
antipattern.reason: In _parallel_execution, all execute_assignment coroutines run concurrently. Any mutable Python object shared by reference between agent handlers (not passed as message content) is subject to concurrent writes without locking. Keep all inter-agent data in Message objects routed through the bus.

antipattern.description: Deadlocks in async message-bus designs when agents wait on each other circularly.
antipattern.reason: Agent A sends to agent B and awaits reply; agent B's handler sends back to agent A before returning. With the default single-event-loop asyncio, this creates a deadlock. Use asyncio.wait_for with timeouts and ensure handlers never await a response from the sender.

section.name: pattern-selection-guide
section.p: Strategy selection guide for production systems:

list.item: Sequential: linear workflows with low complexity where each agent's output is the next agent's input — cheapest, most predictable.
Parallel: independent subtasks with no inter-dependency — highest throughput, requires asyncio.gather with timeout guards.
Hierarchical: complex projects with dependent subtasks — medium complexity, produces traceable assignment log, two extra LLM calls (plan + synthesis).

section.name: production-gotchas
section.p: Production-specific failure modes for message-bus systems:

list.item: Cost explosion: N agents × M strategies = N×M LLM calls; set per-agent and per-run token budgets; abort the run if budget is exceeded.

Human-in-loop checkpoint: insert an explicit approval gate before any agent executes irreversible actions (send email, write to DB, deploy code). The orchestrator must pause and surface the proposed action before proceeding.
Cascading hallucinations: if a researcher worker fabricates a fact, all downstream workers build on it. Add a fact-check node that validates key claims before passing them to the next stage.
Token attribution: in a 10-agent run it is hard to know which agent consumed most tokens. Use agentops or LangSmith tagging per agent role from the start.

section.name: observability-tools
section.p: Essential observability tools for production multi-agent systems:

list.item: agentops (pip install agentops): cost and trace SDK; tag per agent role to attribute token usage per run.
LangSmith (SaaS): traces inter-agent messages; indispensable for debugging orchestration failures.
LangGraph Cloud (SaaS): hosted, persistent multi-agent state with checkpointing — use for long-running production runs.
CrewAI Enterprise (SaaS): managed crew execution with role-based access control.
